description:

They call out Arizona State’s Ilia (Ilya) for painfully slow turns desp...
transcript:
Dax & Elvis go OFF on terrible starts, turns, and technique in college swimming — especially after watching conference meets.
They call out Arizona State’s Ilia (Ilya) for painfully slow turns despite being unstoppable, roast Big 12 teams for the worst execution they've seen, and explain why Tennessee dominates relay starts with low-swing athletic dives (not bunny hops or flopping in).

title: The NCAA Swimming Talent Gap is INSANE: Why UVA, Texas & Cal Dominate  And Most Teams Can't Catch Up
description: Dax & Elvis break down the massive talent gap in NCAA swimming that's leaving most teams in the dust. On the women's side: UVA is in a league of their own, with Texas a distant second and everyone ...
transcript:
Dax & Elvis break down the massive talent gap in NCAA swimming that's leaving most teams in the dust. On the women's side: UVA is in a league of their own, with Texas a distant second and everyone else way behind. Men's side? Texas and Cal run the show, and the rest are playing catch-up forever.
We discuss why it's so hard to close:

Resources (scholarships, facilities, money talks – especially for internationals)
Location (good weather + prestige = recruits flock to Florida, Texas, Cal, UVA)
Elite coaching & recruiting cycles (chicken-or-egg dominance)
Mentality & racer mindset (top teams recruit fearless competitors, not just fast bodies)
Real talk on building programs: Throw money at stars, pray the rest step up... or get stuck supplicating to land anyone.

Stories include: Why Hawaii could dominate but doesn't, Indian River as a feeder for internationals, full-ride demands from Caribbean families, and how track athletes go anywhere for money while swimmers chase specific coaches/programs.
Perfect for swim nerds, college recruits, parents, and fans wondering why the same teams win every year. NCAA 2026 champs approaching – UVA women projected to score 569+ points (235 ahead of #2)!

description: Unfiltered SEC Championships recap on Night Swim Podcast! Dax Hill (Texas Longhorns legend, NCAA champ) and Elvis Burrows break down the "Texas Invite" that is now the SECs. Josh Liendo dominates w...
transcript:

title: UVA Women CRUSH ACCs Again! Stanford's 371-Point Beatdown + Ledecky Dominance & NCAA Power Shifts
description: Dax & Elvis break down the UVA women's swimming team's dominant 7th straight ACC Championship win — crushing Stanford by over 370 points (1410.5 to 1039) and Cal too! We dive into Katie/Kaylee Lede...
transcript:
Dax & Elvis break down the UVA women's swimming team's dominant 7th straight ACC Championship win — crushing Stanford by over 370 points (1410.5 to 1039) and Cal too! We dive into Katie/Kaylee Ledecky's insane distance swims (those sub-15:45 vibes are back), why it's not "just another Ledecky win," and the bigger picture: Is Stanford's dynasty over? What's behind UVA's steamroll era after Stanford ruled with Simone Manuel, Lia Neal, and more?
We talk cyclical nature of college swimming power (Auburn, Texas, Kentucky examples), recruiting snowball effects, fast swimmers eating scholarships, coaching impact (Bob Bowman at ASU vibes), and program identities — like Texas owning the 200 free vs UVA's hodgepodge depth (or lack thereof in some events). Plus, first-ever night swims, freestyle technique from David Marsh, and tips for coaches to challenge UVA by owning an event!

transcript:

Freshman standout Max Williamson (UVA) drops bombs in the 200 IM (silver medal, school record vibes) and hits A-cuts to lock in NCAA spots—high school 41.0 sprinter turning heads. They dive deep into whether Todd DeSorbo's style is clicking on the men's side (UVA men finish 7th at 577.5 while women dominate), the masculine vs. feminine coaching debate, and why some programs favor one gender. Shoutouts to Kai Winkler (NC State tall-boy sprinter stepping up), Jeremy Kelly (Dax's old Zones kid grinding through transfers), and Stanford's close 1076 vs. Cal's winning 1154 (fluke or revival?). title: China Pays $20k TO SHOW UP
description: Plus prize money!

title: China Pays Swimmers $20K Just to Show Up – USA Swimming Exposed!
description: China is shaking up swimming with MASSIVE payouts at the 2026 China Open in Shenzhen – up to $20,000 appearance fees just for showing up, plus $10K for 1st, $8K for 2nd, $6K for 3rd, flights, and h...
transcript:
China is shaking up swimming with MASSIVE payouts at the 2026 China Open in Shenzhen – up to $20,000 appearance fees just for showing up, plus $10K for 1st, $8K for 2nd, $6K for 3rd, flights, and hotels covered for top international stars!

In this episode of Night Swim Podcast, we break down why this meet is a game-changer: elite athletes getting real money, fast exciting racing with only the best, fan experiences, autographs, and star power – while USA Swimming sticks to tiny $2K prizes that barely cover travel.
We rant about:

How USA Swimming & World Aquatics are greedy or incompetent
Why they never capitalized on Phelps-level crowds or pushed stars like Dressel properly
The boring structure of most meets vs. China's smart, short, high-value format
Business lessons: spend money to make money, pay for star power, give fans the experience they want
Marketing fails, "funnest sport" nonsense, and why outsiders have to fix this sport

What do YOU think? Is China saving swimming or just exposing how broken the system is? Drop your take in the comments! 🔥

title: Can Ryan Murphy Medal in LA 2028?
description: Is he focusing more on 50/100?

title: Ryan Murphy is TRAINING - time to get the United States of Backstroke back on track
description: Ryan Murphy is back in the pool training for the LA 2028 Olympics after a year+ break — but is this comeback realistic? In this raw clip, we break down why his weight room focus might be holding hi...
transcript:
Ryan Murphy is back in the pool training for the LA 2028 Olympics after a year+ break — but is this comeback realistic? In this raw clip, we break down why his weight room focus might be holding him back, why getting "too big" could kill his chances at sub-52 in the 100 back, and why historic swimming comebacks (Dara Torres, Anthony Ervin, Dana Vollmer) are super rare — especially for world record holders.
We debate:

His peak WR time (51.85 relay split / 51.97 race) vs. current young guns like Will McGlothen rising fast
The mental game: Stop chasing your "old self" — build a new baseline
Pilates/yoga + technique greater than more muscle for backstroke speed
Odds: Top 8 at Trials? Top 2 for the team? Or 4th place fade?

If you're a swim nerd, this one's for you — brutal honesty on one of America's greatest backstrokers chasing home soil glory. What do you think — can he medal again?

title: Practice vs Meets: Which REALLY Matters More in Swimming?
description:
In this heated discussion, we dive deep into one of the biggest debates in competitive swimming: Does practice matter more than meets, or do meets matter more than practice?
We break down real scen...
transcript:
In this heated discussion, we dive deep into one of the biggest debates in competitive swimming: Does practice matter more than meets, or do meets matter more than practice?
We break down real scenarios:

Can crushing 51s in practice for hours make a 51 LCM 100 free "easy" at taper?
Why grinding 19.9s for two hours translates better to dropping to 19.0 than endless 51s do for sprinting.
The myth of "practice warriors" — kids who dominate practice but disappear on race day.
Why simulating meets in practice (suit-up Saturdays, race-pace Fridays) is game-changing.
One coach's story of racing EVERY meet one summer... and getting FASTER with LESS training volume.

We explore energy systems, race specificity, bringing the meet into practice (vs practice into the meet), and why true elites often underperform in practice but shine when it counts.
Whether you're a swimmer, parent, or coach, this episode will make you rethink how you train and race. Drop your thoughts below: Are you a practice warrior or a meet performer? Which side are you on — practice greater than meets or meets greater than practice?

Michael Andrew joins Dax & Elvis on Night Swim for his most open interview yet.
The Olympic gold medalist opens up about:
• Leaving Arizona State University after one year and returning to train full-time in Hawaii with his dad (Peter Andrew) using USRPT roots
• Emotional low after missing Paris 2024, family challenges, and rebuilding mentally
• LA 2028 goals: 50s events (breast, fly, free), 100 breast, possible 100 fly + relays
• Thoughts on Ilya joining Team USA, racing Shane Casas & the new sprint landscape
• The vaccine controversy, death threats, media hits, and how he built unbreakable mental armor
• Why he skipped the 200 IM forever after Tokyo scars + memes

• Pro swimming reality: ISL experience (no owed money), why it failed, future pro teams, CSL thoughts
